instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

text_blocks.text: Programs are loaded into memory by the operating system.

runs.text: Programs are loaded into memory by the operating system.

text_blocks.text: They have to exist in memory before they can be executed.

runs.text: They have to exist in memory before they can be executed.

text_blocks.text: Programs go through a lot of trouble to have all their data/code in memory.

runs.text: Programs go through a lot of trouble to have all their data/code in memory.

text_blocks.text: Programs are told that they are the only things running…

runs.text: Programs are told that they are the only things running…

text_blocks.text: We know that this is not true!

runs.text: We know that this is not true!

text_blocks.text: Operating Systems are big liars crafting illusions.

runs.text: Operating Systems are big liars crafting illusions.

text_blocks.text: In reality, many programs can be running at the same time.

runs.text: In reality, many programs can be running at the same time.

text_blocks.text: Each program, when running, is typically called a process.

runs.text: Each program, when running, is typically called a

text_blocks.text: A multitasking OS is (a rather common) one that supports concurrent processes.

runs.text: OS is (a rather common) one that supports concurrent processes.

text_blocks.text: The OS must handle switching from one process to another.

runs.text: The OS must handle switching from one process to another.

text_blocks.text: What if you have more processes than CPUs?

runs.text: What if you have more processes than CPUs?

text_blocks.text: When do you switch from one to another?

runs.text: When do you switch from one to another?

text_blocks.text: What if one is more urgent??

runs.text: What if one is more urgent??

input.title: My process is one of method…

text_blocks.text: My process is one of method…

runs.text: My process is one of method…

text_blocks.text: A process is an abstraction representing a single instance of a program.
An executable represents the initial state of a program and thus the process.

runs.text: An executable represents the initial state of a program and thus the process.

text_blocks.text: A program can be instantiated multiple times, if needed.

runs.text: A program can be instantiated multiple times, if needed.

text_blocks.text: Each one would be a separate process… of the same program.

runs.text: Each one would be a separate process… of the same program.

text_blocks.text: Note: A processor is the hardware unit that executes a process. (makes sense!!)

runs.text: is the hardware unit that executes a process. (makes sense!!)

text_blocks.text: The Operating System defines what a process and its abstraction is.

runs.text: The Operating System defines what a process and its abstraction is.

text_blocks.text: There is an OS representation and metadata associated with a process.

runs.text: There is an OS representation and metadata associated with a process.

text_blocks.text: The OS maintains two key lies:
The control flow (exclusive use of CPU): as defined by the code (this lecture)

runs.text: The control flow (exclusive use of CPU): as defined by the code (this lecture)

text_blocks.text: The memory layout (exclusive use of memory): defined by executable/code (next lecture)

runs.text: The memory layout (exclusive use of memory): defined by executable/code (next lecture)

text_blocks.text: We are focusing on the control flow, here.

runs.text: We are focusing on the control flow, here.

text_blocks.text: How do we determine when a program runs? When does the lie… break down?

runs.text: How do we determine when a program runs? When does the lie… break down?

text_blocks.text: Let us say that we have a machine with four separate CPUs.

runs.text: Let us say that we have a machine with four separate CPUs.

text_blocks.text: You could run four processes concurrently (at the same time) relatively easily.

runs.text: You could run four processes concurrently (at the same time) relatively easily.

text_blocks.text: Truth be told, we often have fewer resources than needed.

runs.text: Truth be told, we often have fewer resources than needed.

text_blocks.text: Sharing a common resource is called multiplexing.

runs.text: Sharing a common resource is called

text_blocks.text: Now, consider a machine with a single CPU.

runs.text: Now, consider a machine with a single CPU.

text_blocks.text: We often want to run something in the foreground.

runs.text: We often want to run something in the foreground.

text_blocks.text: Word processor, web browser, minesweeper… whatever.

runs.text: Word processor, web browser, minesweeper… whatever.

text_blocks.text: We still want some things running the background…

runs.text: We still want some things running the background…

text_blocks.text: Music player, virus scanner, chat client.

runs.text: Music player, virus scanner, chat client.

text_blocks.text: Yet that’s not very flexible. (Stop your music player to open a PDF)

runs.text: Humans are in the mix! We need computers to be useful to us.

text_blocks.text: To multiplex the CPU, we quickly switch from process to process.

runs.text: To multiplex the CPU, we quickly switch from process to process.

text_blocks.text: The OS retains/restores the state (context) of the process.
The OS must store this as a form of process metadata in memory.

runs.text: The OS must store this as a form of process metadata in memory.

text_blocks.text: When an Operating System goes from one process to another, it performs a context switch.

runs.text: When an Operating System goes from one process to another, it performs a

text_blocks.text: This swaps out the CPU state of one process for the next one to run.

runs.text: This swaps out the CPU state of one process for the next one to run.

text_blocks.text: Store registers (including stack pointer and program counter) to memory.

runs.text: Store registers (including stack pointer and program counter) to memory.

text_blocks.text: Load those registers from memory. Switch memory space. (see next lecture: virtual memory)

runs.text: Load those registers from memory. Switch memory space.

text_blocks.text: Jump to old program counter. Go!

runs.text: Jump to old program counter. Go!

text_blocks.text: When we pause a process… we store the state of registers (context)

runs.text: When we pause a process… we store the state of registers (context)

runs.text: When should a program pause and let another one go?

text_blocks.text: When programs voluntarily pause, this is called cooperative scheduling.

text_blocks.text: They may give up control at convenient points such as system calls.

runs.text: They may give up control at convenient points such as system calls.

text_blocks.text: We often do not expect this, so modern Operating Systems forcibly pause programs from time to time. Called preemptive scheduling.

runs.text: We often do not expect this, so modern Operating Systems forcibly pause programs from time to time. Called

text_blocks.text: Processes give up control when hardware interjects via an “interrupt”

runs.text: Processes give up control when hardware interjects via an “interrupt”

text_blocks.text: One method is to just cycle through each process each for equal time.

runs.text: One method is to just cycle through each process each for equal time.

text_blocks.text: This is an element of “fairness” … each gets equal stake.

runs.text: This is an element of “fairness” … each gets equal stake.

runs.text: In round-robin, I give the video game 25% of my resources.

text_blocks.text: Like many of the topics in this course, there is no possible “best”.

runs.text: Like many of the topics in this course,

text_blocks.text: That is, there is no way to perfectly schedule general processes.

runs.text: no way to perfectly schedule general processes

text_blocks.text: Consider: It would be very lovely to schedule a process that handles some user input, like a button press or a network request.

runs.text: Consider: It would be very lovely to schedule a process that handles some user input, like a button press or a network request.

text_blocks.text: Perfect situation: the OS schedules the task that handles the button immediately before the button is pressed. What luck!

runs.text: Perfect situation: the OS schedules the task that handles the button immediately before the button is pressed. What luck!

text_blocks.text: However: You do not know when that button will be pressed.

runs.text: However: You do not know when that button will be pressed.

text_blocks.text: Maybe it is a sensor, like for detecting a fire!

runs.text: Maybe it is a sensor, like for detecting a fire!

text_blocks.text: Moral of the story: humans being users make things very hard.

runs.text: Moral of the story: humans being users make things very hard.

text_blocks.text: But… wait… how does hardware stop a program?
For instance, when the quantum is up, how does the OS get control and perform the context switch?

runs.text: For instance, when the quantum is up, how does the OS get control and perform the context switch?

text_blocks.text: Ah, the hardware has support for “being rude” which is called an interrupt.

runs.text: Ah, the hardware has support for “being rude” which is called an interrupt.

text_blocks.text: A programmable mechanism for asynchronously calling a function when a particular type of error or signal is noticed.

runs.text: A programmable mechanism for asynchronously calling a function when a particular type of error or signal is noticed.

text_blocks.text: An interrupt is an exceptional state that diverts execution from its normal flow.

runs.text: is an exceptional state that diverts execution from its normal flow.

text_blocks.text: When issued by hardware, sometimes referred to as a hardware exception

runs.text: When issued by hardware, sometimes referred to as a

text_blocks.text: For instance, a hardware timer or external event caused by a sensor.

runs.text: For instance, a hardware timer or external event caused by a sensor.

text_blocks.text: When caused by a user process, sometimes referred to as a software trap

runs.text: When caused by a user process, sometimes referred to as a

text_blocks.text: Divide-by-zero error, some floating-point exceptions, system calls.

runs.text: Divide-by-zero error, some floating-point exceptions, system calls.

text_blocks.text: System calls are a type of interrupt (software trap).

runs.text: are a type of interrupt (software trap).

text_blocks.text: This is an intentional interrupt caused by a specific program instruction.

runs.text: This is an intentional interrupt caused by a specific program instruction.

text_blocks.text: The program is “interrupted” while the OS performs a task.

runs.text: The program is “interrupted” while the OS performs a task.

text_blocks.text: We have also encountered them in our failures.

runs.text: We have also encountered them in our failures.

text_blocks.text: Segmentation / Protection / Page Faults are also interrupts. (trap? exception?)
These are (usually) unintentional interrupts caused by a generic instruction.

runs.text: These are (usually) unintentional interrupts caused by a generic instruction.

text_blocks.text: A hardware timer can preempt (forcibly pause) a program at any time.

runs.text: A hardware timer can preempt (forcibly pause) a program at any time.

text_blocks.text: Interrupts that occur at any instruction are asynchronous interrupts.

runs.text: Interrupts that occur at any instruction are

text_blocks.text: In a preemptive operating system, a hardware timer is used to give a maximum bound to how long a process runs.

runs.text: In a preemptive operating system, a hardware timer is used to give a maximum bound to how long a process runs.

text_blocks.text: Your operating system programs the timer such that it sends a signal at a regular interval.

runs.text: Your operating system programs the timer such that it sends a signal at a regular interval.

text_blocks.text: Your operating system has a function that is called when such a signal is read.

runs.text: Your operating system has a function that is called when such a signal is read.

text_blocks.text: That function will respond by invoking the scheduler and pausing the current task and resuming or starting another.

runs.text: That function will respond by invoking the scheduler and pausing the current task and resuming or starting another.

text_blocks.text: Let’s look at the basic procedure an OS uses to program an interrupt.

runs.text: Let’s look at the basic procedure an OS uses to program an interrupt.

text_blocks.text: On most hardware, there is a programmable table somewhere in memory that, when written to, defines where code exist to handle each interrupt.

runs.text: On most hardware, there is a programmable table somewhere in memory that, when written to, defines where code exist to handle each interrupt.

text_blocks.text: Every possible interrupt is given a number. Segmentation faults might be interrupt 10. Timers might be interrupt 0. Et cetera.

runs.text: Every possible interrupt is given a number. Segmentation faults might be interrupt 10. Timers might be interrupt 0. Et cetera.

text_blocks.text: When an interrupt occurs, based on its interrupt number, the corresponding entry in a lookup table called an interrupt vector table or an interrupt descriptor table would be used to determine where in the kernel to jump.

runs.text: When an interrupt occurs, based on its interrupt number, the corresponding entry in a lookup table called an
would be used to determine where in the kernel to jump.

text_blocks.text: The interrupt table is a simple table.

runs.text: The interrupt table is a simple table.

text_blocks.text: Fun Fact: It is often located at address 0x0 in memory!

runs.text: Fun Fact: It is often located at address 0x0 in memory!

text_blocks.text: So, operating system kernels can’t exactly always treat zero as an invalid address…

runs.text: So, operating system kernels can’t exactly always treat zero as an invalid address…

text_blocks.text: When a process triggers a listed interrupt or external hardware sends a signal to the interrupt controller…

runs.text: When a process triggers a listed interrupt or external hardware sends a signal to the interrupt controller…
